coredata.dc:description: The effective detection of biomarkers associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is of great importance. Golgi protein 73 (GP73), a serum biomarker of HCC, has better diagnostic value than Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) has been reported. In this paper, highly accurate fluorescence sensing platform for detecting GP73 was constructed based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), in which nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) labelling with GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt) was used as fluorescence probe, and molybdenum disulfide @ reduced graphene oxide (MoS2@RGO) nanosheets was used as fluorescent receptors. MoS2@RGO nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of NGQDs-GP73Apt owing to FRET mechanisms. In the presence of GP73, the NGQDs-GP73Apt specifically bound with GP73 to from the deployable structures, making NGQDs-GP73Apt far away from MoS2@RGO nanosheets, blocking the FRET process, resulting in fluorescence recovery of NGQDs-GP73Apt. Under optimal conditions, the recovery intensity of fluorescence in the detection system is linearly related to the concentration of GP73 in the range of 5 ng/mL − 100 ng/mL and the limit of detection is 4.54 ng/mL (S/N = 3). Moreover, detection of GP73 was performed in human serum samples with good recovery (97.21–100.83%). This platform provides a feasible method for the early diagnosis of HCC, and can be easily extended to the detection of other biomarkers.

(E) The X-ray diffraction spectra of MoS2@RGO nanosheets and PDF standard card of MoS2. (F) The XPS characterization of MoS2@RGO nanosheets.

(B) Fluorescence intensity recovery of NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system with different concentrations of GP73 (a) no GP73; (b) 500 ng/mL GP73; (c) 1000 ng/mL GP73.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent strategy for Golgi protein 73 determination is constructed based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), in which GP73 aptamer modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs-GP73Apt) is used as fluorescent donor and MoS2@RGO nanosheets as fluorescent receptor. The fluorescence platform has wide output range, low cost, good specificity and recovery for GP73 determination.

The effective detection of biomarkers associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is of great importance. Golgi protein 73 (GP73), a serum biomarker of HCC, has better diagnostic value than Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) has been reported. In this paper, highly accurate fluorescence sensing platform for detecting GP73 was constructed based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), in which nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) labelling with GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt) was used as fluorescence probe, and molybdenum disulfide @ reduced graphene oxide (MoS2@RGO) nanosheets was used as fluorescent receptors. MoS2@RGO nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of NGQDs-GP73Apt owing to FRET mechanisms. In the presence of GP73, the NGQDs-GP73Apt specifically bound with GP73 to from the deployable structures, making NGQDs-GP73Apt far away from MoS2@RGO nanosheets, blocking the FRET process, resulting in fluorescence recovery of NGQDs-GP73Apt. Under optimal conditions, the recovery intensity of fluorescence in the detection system is linearly related to the concentration of GP73 in the range of 5 ng/mL − 100 ng/mL and the limit of detection is 4.54 ng/mL (S/N = 3). Moreover, detection of GP73 was performed in human serum samples with good recovery (97.21–100.83%). This platform provides a feasible method for the early diagnosis of HCC, and can be easily extended to the detection of other biomarkers.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Molybdenum disulfide @ reduced graphene oxide nanosheets

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is one of the most common causes of cancer-related death worldwide [1], largely because ‘one-size-fits-all’ HCC screening approaches recommended by clinical practice guidelines for early tumor detection are used in less than 20% of the population [2], resulting in poor early diagnosis effect. The most common biomarker for the detection of cancer in clinical practice is alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), which is used in combination with imaging methods such as ultrasound, but its effectiveness is limited due to its low sensitivity and specificity, as well as the differences between different detection methods; and the accuracy of AFP results may also vary depending on patient characteristics and the AFP threshold used [3]. So, a new biomarker with good selectivity and specificity is needed for the diagnosis of HCC. Golgi protein 73 (GP73), a glycosylated protein located in the Golgi cisternae, is generally recognized as an ideal serum marker for the early diagnosis and evaluation of postoperative recurrence of hepatocellular carcinoma, especially HCC [4]. O. M. Ali et al. [5] have performed a case-control study about the difference between AFP and GP73 in the diagnosis of HCC, involving a total of 60 patients (30 HCC patients with either chronic HBV or HCV infection and 30 non-HCC patients with HVB or HCV infection who had either chronic hepatitis or liver cirrhosis) and 30 healthy volunteers. The results show that the sensitivity of GP73 in differentiating HCC patients from normal population (cut-off value of GP73 was 8.4 ng/mL) and HCC patients from non-HCC patients (the value was 8.45 ng/mL) was 86.7% and 83.3%, respectively; and the corresponding sensitivity of AFP were 75.4% (cut-off value of AFP was 2.4 ng/mL) and 72.2% (cut-off value of AFP was 20.85 ng/mL). The data indicate that the diagnostic efficacy of GP73 is higher than AFP, and the diagnostic value of GP73 in HCC is higher than that in other cancers. Therefore, GP73 has a high diagnostic value as a biomarker of HCC.

At present, the detection methods of GP73 include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), chemiluminescence assay, electrochemical method and so on [6–11]. However, these methods have strict requirements for reaction conditions, low sensitivity and high cost, which have limited further clinical application of them [12]. Fluorescence analysis is an advanced analytical method with high analytical sensitivity, selectivity and ease of use. It allows qualitative or quantitative analysis of substances with respect to changes in photophysical properties such as changes in fluorescence intensity, emission spectra and the lifetime of fluorescent molecules [13]. Particularly, fluorescent biosensors have been developed to detect cancer markers in recent years based on the change of fluorescence signal before and after the interaction between aptamer and target molecules. For example, W. Liu et al. [14] have proposed the fluorescent biosensor for GP73 detection by using fluorescence quenching and recovery of fluorescent molecules (Mn-modified CdTe/Dds quantum dots), with a detection range of 20 to 150 ng/mL and the limit of detection of 10 ng/mL.

Based on different photophysical processes, the mechanisms of fluorescence signal quenching and recovery mainly include photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [15], internal filtration effect (IFE) [16], intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [17], aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [18,19] and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [20]. Among them, FRET has been widely applied as important sensing mode based on the fluorescent quenching or enhancement mechanism. FRET occurred in two different fluorophore electronic excited state between excited state energy transferred from a fluorophore donor to a proximal ground-state acceptor by a dipole–dipole coupling [13]. In the other words, the non-radiative energy of the fluorescence donor from the excited state to the ground state is absorbed by the fluorescence acceptor, so that the fluorescence phenomenon generated by the energy emission is not produced and the effect of fluorescence quench is achieved. Compared with other fluorescence analysis methods, FRET analysis has been widely used to study molecular interactions occurring at close distances in biology due to its inherent sensitivity to nanoscale changes in the distance from donors to acceptors [21,22]. For the detection of biomarkers, fluorescence-based FRET analysis has the advantages of high sensitivity, fast reaction speed and low cost compared with traditional detection methods such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR), immunohistochemistry and flow cytometry [23,24]. Mohamad Mahani et al. [25] designed a label-free and specific FRET-based aptasensor to determine interleukin-6 (IL-6) in human serum with a fluorescence donor of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) and a fluorescence receptor of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The results display a linear response of 1.5–5.9 pg/mL with a detection limit of 0.82 pg/mL. However, conventional FRET techniques, which are mainly used down-converting fluorophores such as organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots as energy donors, are usually subject to background interference from the biological sample matrix under UV–vis photoexcitation [26]. Therefore, the choice of fluorescent material is very important.

At present, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have been used as an outstanding fluorescence donor for FRET analysis in comparison with the conventional fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots because of their high electron and hole migration rates, electron transfer speed, water solubility, high photostability and good biocompatibility [27]. However, the complex surface chemical properties of GQDs hinder the control of their photoluminescence and quantum yield [28]. The optical and electronic properties of GQDs can be improved by adding appropriate reagents for surface functionalization or hybrid atom doping in the process of preparing GQDs [29]. So far, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been selected as a better scheme to improve the optical and electronic properties of GQDs due to its easy synthesis, green environmental protection, strong electron transfer ability and large specific surface area [30–32]. Since N atoms have a considerable atomic size and five unpaired electrons, the band gap of GQDs can be adjusted by nitrogen doping with GQDs. Nitrogen doping can change the density of electron clouds on the surface of GQDs and regulate their physicochemical properties, making them highly fluorescent stable and less prone to bleaching, and therefore also less prone to serious fluorescence decay [33]. Therefore, NGQDs have a satisfactory effect as fluorescence donors of FRET analysis system. For example, Z. M. Li et al.[34] have developed a rapid and sensitive fluorescence assay based on FRET between NGQDs and manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanocomposites for detecting glutathione (GSH) concentration in human serum with the detection range of 0–400 M, indicating a broad prospect of NGQDs as a fluorescent material.

In addition, the FRET quenching mechanism has been widely applied in the development of biosensors through integration with nanostructured material, such as graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (RGO), molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) manganese dioxide (MnO2) and so on, due to their efficient fluorescence quenching ability [35]. Among them, RGO is one of the most commonly fluorescent quencher because of good electron mobility, non-toxicity, extraordinary chemical stability and high electron density [36]. Moreover, RGO shows satisfactory quenching performance because it has stronger π-π conjugate structure and lower surface charge density to adsorb single-stranded DNA or aptamers closely [37]. H. X. Wang et al. [38] designed a highly sensitive fluorescent aptasensor for glypican-3 (GPC3, another HCC biomarker) detection stemmed from the principle of FRET with RGO as its fluorescence quench agent. The detection range was 5–150 ng/mL and the limit of detection was 2.395 ng/mL. At the same time, MoS2 nanosheets, a kind of two-dimensional layer structure like RGO, also has higher specific surface area and excellent fluorescence quenching ability because of its wide absorption band from 200 to 800 nm, good electron mobility, and high electron density [39]. According to a review study by Yao et al, MoS2 nanosheets have been used as a highly efficient fluorescence quenching agent for the detection of a variety of biological analytes since the discovery of the strong adsorption capacity of MoS2 nanosheets for single-stranded DNA [40]. More importantly, compared with GO and MoS2 nanosheets, molybdenum disulfide@reduced graphene oxide (MoS2@RGO) nanosheets has better fluorescence quenching due to the synergistic interaction between RGO and MoS2 because MoS2 and RGO have similar layered structures and good quenching effect [41,42]. R. Ge et al. [41] mentioned that RGO could modulates the morphology of MoS2, facilitating the generation and migration of charge marriers, and used MoS2@RGO to establish a biofriendly photoelectrochemical platform for the analysis of Escherichia coli O 157: H7. The higher electron transfer rate of MoS2@RGO than MoS2 and RGO determines the better quenching effect of MoS2@RGO, which is analyzed from the principle of FRET. In addition, based on the analysis of the non-radiative resonance energy transfer from quantum dots to graphene and MoS2 by Archana Jaja et al. [42], it can be deduced that the energy transfer rate of MoS2@RGO is affected after the combination of RGO and MoS2, which is another reason why the quenching effect of MoS2@RGO is better than that of MoS2 and RGO. So, it is reasonable and innovative to develop a fluorescence quenching-based FRET platform for GP73 detection using MoS2@RGO nanosheets as fluorescence receptor.

Herein, a fluorescence sensing platform for detecting GP73 had developed based on the FRET process between NGQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2@RGO nanosheets and the interaction between GP73 protein and GP73Apt. Aminated GP73Apt was combined with NGQDs to form fluorescence NGQDs-GP73Apt probe. MoS2@RGO nanosheets have better quenching performance as the fluorescent receptor for this platform due to the synergistic interaction between RGO and MoS2. Due to the van der Waals force and hydrogen bonding effect of MoS2@RGO nanosheets to aptamer molecules (ssDNA), the nucleic acid bases in aptamer can be physically adsorbed on MoS2@RGO nanosheets, thus quenching the fluorescence of NGQDs-GP73Apt thanks to FRET mechanism. In the presence of GP73, the NGQDs-GP73Apt specifically bound with GP73 to from the deployable structures, making NGQDs-GP73Apt far away from MoS2@RGO nanosheets, blocking the FRET process, resulting in fluorescence recovery of NGQDs-GP73Apt. The concentration of GP73 was quantified detected by recording the variation in fluorescence intensity derived from the fluorescence sensing platform by spectrophotometer. The fluorescence sensing platform based on FRET system can provide a feasible solution for detecting GP73 and other human serum markers.

Immunoglobulin G (IgG), Bovine serum albumin(BSA), Human serum albumin (HSA), Immunoglobulin E (IgE), GP73 protein and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China, https://www.aladdin-e.com/). Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) was purchased from Xianfeng Nano Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China, https://www.xfnano.com/). Poly-vinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) and ascorbic acid were produced by Beijing solarbio science & technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China, https://www.solarbio.com/); and 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was provided by Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Dalian, China, https://www.meilune.com/). The aminated GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt) with the following sequences (5′–NH2–C6-GCAGTTGATCCTTTGGATACCCTGG-3′)[43] was purchased from Shenggong Bioengineering (Shanghai) Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China, https://www.sangon.com/).

Fluorescence spectra were performed with a F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan, https://www.shimadzu.com/). The MoS2@RGO were characterized by Ultraviolet visible spectroscopy (UV–vis, UH-5300, Hitachi Company, Japan, https://www.hitachi.com/), transmission electron microscopy and high precision transmission electron microscopy (TEM and HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, USA, https://www.fei.com/), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker Tensor 27, Germany, https://www.bruker.com/), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab 250Xi, Thermofisher, USA, https://www.thermofisher.cn) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 200, FEI Company, USA, https://www.fei.com/).

First, 20 mg of MoS2 was dissolved with 20 mL ultrapure water and crushed for 1 h by an ultrasonic cell crusher to obtain MoS2 solution. RGO solution was prepared according to method of X. Shi et al [44]. Second, 100 mg PVP and MoS2 solution were dissolved in RGO solution, and stirred overnight (12 h) with a stirrer to ensure MoS2 was heavily piled on the RGO.Third, the completely stirred solution was separated by centrifugal at 8000 rpm for 8 min to obtain the precipitate. Last, the lower precipitate was put into an electric blast drying oven and dried for 6 h at 80 °C to obtain MoS2@RGO nanosheets powder.

First, 10 mg NGQDs powder was dissolved in the 10 mL PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) and diluted to 8 μg/mL, then the NGQDs solution was mixed with EDC (1.0 mg/mL) at a volume ratio of 10:3 by ultrasound for 40 min to activate the carboxyl group on NGQDs. Second, 100 μL 1.0 μM aminated GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt) was added to 100 μL activated NGQDs solution at 25 °C for 60 min to obtain NGQDs-GP73Apt fluorescence probe. The emission spectrum of NGQDs-GP73Apt was measured at a wavelength of 310 nm to obtain the fluorescence intensity.

The conditions, such as the concentration of GP73 aptamer, pH of the PBS buffer solution, incubation time and incubation temperature, in the construction of NGQDs-GP73Apt was optimized, respectively. The corresponding optimization results under the above four experimental conditions were 1.0 μM, pH = 7.0, 60 min, and 25℃ (Fig.S1).

First, 5.0 mg MoS2@RGO nanosheets was mixed with 5 mL ultrapure water and make it homogeneous with an oscillator. Second, 20 μL NGQDs-GP73Apt solution and 20 μL MoS2@RGO solution (800 μg/mL) were mixed and stirred 25 °C for 20 min. After incubation, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO was measured (denoted as F0) under λex/em of 310/395 nm and compared with that of N-GQDs-GP73Apt and N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of determination of GP73 with the fluorescence sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: First, the GP73 protein (2.0 μg/mL) was diluted into 10 group (5, 8, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 500 ng/mL). Fluorescence intensity of the above NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO solution was measured at the condition of λex/em = 310/395 nm, which was marked as F0. Second, 50 μL NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO solution was mixed with 150 μL buffer solution (pH = 7.0). Third, 100 μL GP73 protein solution with different concentrations was added to 200 μL of the diluted NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO solution and incubated in a 25 °C incubator for 60 min. The fluorescence intensity for different GP73-NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO solution were record under λex/em of 310/400 nm (denoted as F1). The relative fluorescence recovery was calculated using the formula (F1-F0)/F0. Finally, the relationship curve was established with (F1-F0)/F0 as the vertical coordinate and the concentration of GP73 as the horizontal coordinate, so that the detection of unknown concentration of GP73 was realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GP73 in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum samples were acquired from Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Diseases Research in 924th Hospital of Chinese People’s Liberation Army (Guilin, China), and followed the requirements of the Ethics Committee of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Diseases in 924th Hospital of the Chinese people's Liberation Army. First, 2.0 μL of GP73 standard solutions of known various concentrations (20, 40, 120 ng/mL) were mixed with 2.0 μL of normal human serum at the volume ratio of 1:1 as spiked samples. Then, the spiked sample (4.0 μL) was mixed with the diluted NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO solution (8.0 μL) and incubated in a 25 °C incubator for 60 min. Lastly, the fluorescence emission was recorded (λex/em = 310/395 nm) three times in parallel measurement. The concentration of GP73 in spiked sample was determined by the calculated recovery.

Fig. 1 A is the SEM characterization of MoS2@RGO nanosheets. As can be seen from Fig. 1A, MoS2@RGO nanosheets is a reticular structure with some spherical network structure attached to its surface. According to the literature [45], it’s obvious that spherical network structure is MoS2 and the reticular structure is RGO, so it was proved that MoS2 had accumulated on the RGO.

Fig. 1B is the TEM image of MoS2@RGO nanosheets. It is the layered structure with other material attached causing the presence of shadows. And the laminar structure is RGO, and the shaded part is caused by the overlap between RGO and MoS2. By observation, the diameter of MoS2@RGO nanosheets is from 380 nm to 600 nm.

Fig. 1C displays the Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) of RGO, MoS2 and MoS2@RGO. The spectrum of RGO (curve a) shows obvious feature peaks at 3411.99 cm−1 (stretching vibration of O–H), 1744.03 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C = O), 1673.01 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C = C), 1386.96 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C–H) and 1112.25 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C–C) [46]. By comparing the spectrum of MoS2@RGO (curve b) with that of MoS2 (curve c), it is found that the positions of characteristic peak of MoS2@RGO (3429.14 cm−1, 1654.51 cm−1, 13390.48 cm−1, 1103.58 cm−1 and 562.07 cm−1) is almost consistent with that of MoS2 (3434.9 cm−1, 1629.92 cm−1, 1398.33 cm−1, 1128.55 cm−1 and 614.22 cm−1). In addition, the spectral comparison between MoS2@RGO and RGO shows that the characteristic peak of C = O disappears, and the characteristic peaks of MoS2@RGO at 1654.51 cm−1 and 1103.58 cm−1 are significantly different from that of RGO at 1673.01 cm−1 and 1112.25 cm−1. The above analysis suggests that MoS2@RGO nanosheets has been successfully prepared.

Fig. 1D is the UV–vis spectra of RGO, MoS2 and MoS2@RGO. The absorption peaks of MoS2@RGO are very obvious (Fig. 1D, curve b), and the peaks are 212.5 nm, 225.0 nm and 262.5 nm respectively. Compared with the absorption peak of RGO (Fig. 1D, curve a), the absorption peak value of MoS2@RGO at 262.5 nm is consistent with that of RGO. Compared with the absorption peaks of the MoS2 (210 nm, 230 nm, Fig. 1D, curve c), the deviation of the two absorption peaks can be observed, but the structure of the absorption peak remains unchanged. Therefore, the UV–vis of MoS2@RGO nanosheets combines the features of MoS2 and RGO.

Fig. 1E is the XRD of MoS2@RGO nanosheets. The MoS2@RGO has obvious diffraction peaks at 14.25°, 39.33°, 43.87°, 49.64°, 59.89° and 88.66°, corresponding to the (002), (103), (006), (105), (008) and (118) crystal planes of MoS2, respectively. And MoS2@RGO has strongest diffraction peak at 14.25°, which is consistent with the MoS2 standard card, which proved that MoS2 was present in the prepared material. According to the literature [47], the diffraction peak degree of RGO is between 20 and 30, and the diffraction peak position of MoS2@RGO in the range of 20 and 30 in the Fig. 1E just corresponds to the diffraction peak of RGO. The Raman characterization (Fig.S2) further confirmed the successful synthesis of MoS2 and RGO.

Fig. 1F shows the analysis of the elements and valence states of MoS2@RGO nanosheets by XPS characterization. The date shows that material has different elemental such as sulfur (25.65%), molybdenum (11.32%), carbon (52.12%) and oxygen (10.09%), which further verified the synthesis of the MoS2@RGO nanosheets. The split-peak fitting plot of MoS2@RGO (Fig.S3) can be clearly showed that there are the presence of C-S bond in the peak of C1s, and the presence of S-O and Mo-O bonds in the peak of O1s.

Fig. 2 A and Fig. 2D show the TEM images of NGQDs and NQGDs-GP73Apt (their high-resolution TEM images (HRTEM) are shown in the upper right inset), respectively. TEM images showed that NGQDs and NGQDs-GP73Apt were spherical in different sizes, but the diameter of NGQDs-GP73Apt was larger than that of NGQDs. In addition, obvious lattice stripes can be seen in the HRTEM of NGQDs (insert of Fig. 2A), and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which is corresponding to the typical lattice plane of graphene (1120) [48]. However, the lattice of NGQDs-GP73Apt in HRTEM image (insert of Fig. 2D) shows that obvious lattice defects in all parts except the white circle in the red circle. These differences also indicate the successful binding of GP73Apt and NGQDs to form NGQDs-GP73Apt.

The fluorescence spectra of NGQDs under different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 2B. NGQDs had a maximum fluorescence intensity of 2628 at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm, corresponding to an emission wavelength of 395 nm. Therefore, all the experiments were carried out under the condition of λex/em = 310/395 nm. The red curve in Fig. 2C shows the emission spectrum of NGQDs at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm, and the black curve shows excitation spectrum with the emission wavelength of 395 nm, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 2B. In addition, NGQDs were colorless and transparent under natural light, while emitting a distinct blue fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation (insert of Fig. 2C), which verified the blue fluorescent nature of NGQDs.

As a molecular marker tool in this experiment, amino modified GP73Apt has the advantages of strong chemical stability and easy modification [49]. The fluorescent probe N-GQDs-GP73Apt is formed by EDC coupled action between the carboxyl group of N-GQDs and the amino dehydration of GP73Apt. Fig. 2E is the fluorescence spectra of NGQDs and NGQDs-GP73Apt. The two solutions have the same shape fluorescence emission spectra and the maximum emission wavelength is at 395 nm. And the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs was 2510, while that of NGQDs-GP73Apt was 2316, indicating that NGQDs was successfully combined with GP73Apt to form NGQDs-GP73Apt. Compared with NGQDs, NGQDs-GP73Apt has more obvious lattice defects (Fig. 2D), resulting in its photoluminescence performance not good than that of NGQDs. In addition, the fluorescence performance of NGQDs-GP73Apt under ultraviolet light is relatively stable with small fluctuations (Fig.S4), which proves the stability of this fluorescence sensing platform.

Fig. 2F shows the fluorescence spectra of NGQDs-GP73Apt (red line) and UV spectra of MoS2@RGO (blue line). The fluorescence emission spectrum of NGQDs-GP73Apt overlaps with the absorption spectrum of MoS2@RGO (blue curve) at 325 nm to 550 nm, which proves that FRET can occur between the NGQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2@RGO nanosheets. When the emission spectrum of fluorescence donor NGQDs-GP73Apt has some overlap with the absorption spectrum of acceptor MoS2@RGO, and the distance between energy donor and energy acceptor is suitable, the fluorescence energy is transferred from donor to acceptor, thus MoS2@RGO quenches the fluorescence of NGQDs-GP73Apt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS characterization of NGQDs and NGQDs-GP73Apt
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A shows the XPS survey of NGQDs-GP73Apt and NGQDs. The NGQDs-GP73Apt and NGQDs contain the same elements but with different contents of each element. For example, the C and N contents of NGQDs-GP73Apt are 63.79% and 13.28%, respectively, which are higher than those of NGQDs (62.14% and 9.79%), but the O content of NGQDs is 28.07%, higher than that of NGQDs-GP73Apt (22.92%). This is because NGQDs combined with GP73Apt by condensation reaction to form NGQDs-GP73Apt so that the C and N contents increased, the O content decreased. This indicates that the NGQDs-GP73Apt is successfully prepared and can be used for subsequent experiments.

Fig. 3B, Fig. 3C and Fig. 3D show the XPS fractional peak fitting plots of NGQDs (lower half of each figure) and NGQDs-GP73Apt (upper half of each figure). Each of the above and below fitted peaks of the same color carries the same bond. In Fig. 3B, the binding energies corresponding to the five peaks of C1s of NGQDs from low to high are 284.01 (C = C), 285.13 (C–C), 286.04 (C-N), 187.24 (C-O) and 287.92 (C = O), respectively. The corresponding five peaks of NGQDs-GP73Apt are 283.94 (C = C), 285.21 (C–C), 285.97 (C-N), 287.06 (C-O) and 287.62 (C = O). The C-N content of NGQDs-GP73Apt is increased compared with NGQDs, which proves the binding of GP73Apt to NGQDs. In Fig. 3C, the binding energies corresponding to the three peaks of N1s of NGQDs from low to high are 398.73 (Pyridine N), 399.36 (Pyrrolic N) and 400.75 (graphitic N), and the corresponding three peaks of NGQDs-GP73Apt are 398.38 (Pyridine N). 399.09 (Pyrrolic N) and 400.93 (graphitic N) [50]. In Fig. 3D, the binding energies corresponding to the four peaks of O1s of NGQDs from low to high are 530.79 (C-OH), 531.38 (C = O), 531.95 (C-O-C) and 532.75 (C-O), respectively. The corresponding peaks of NGQDs-GP73Apt are 530.36 (C-OH), 531.34 (C = O), 531.98 (C-O-C) and 532.8 (C-O), respectively [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical principle of GP73 fluorescence sensing platform based on NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, the fluorescence sensing platform for detection of GP73 based on NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system was constructed as shown in Fig. 4 A. The detection process consisted of two stages: fluorescence quenching and recovering. NGQDs have good lattice fringes and good photoluminescence performance. GP73Apt modified NGQDs (NGQDs-GP73Apt) with high fluorescence efficiency, good stability and high specificity for recognization of GP73 were prepared by the coupling reaction of ultrasonic oscillation in EDC solution. MoS2@RGO nanosheets have better quenching performance as the fluorescent receptor due to the synergistic interaction between RGO and MoS2. In addition, MoS2@RGO nanosheets has a strong electron transfer rate, which provides a guarantee for the quenching efficiency of fluorescence. NGQDs-GP73Apt is able to absorb onto the on MoS2@RGO nanosheets via Van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding effect between GP73Apt and MoS2@RGO, which will lead to fluorescence resonance energy transfer between NGQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2@RGO nanosheets, thus quenching the fluorescence of NGQDs-GP73Apt. In the presence of GP73, the NGQDs-GP73Apt specifically bound with GP73 to from the deployable structures, making NGQDs-GP73Apt far away from MoS2@RGO nanosheets, blocking the FRET process, resulting in fluorescence recovery of NGQDs-GP73Apt. The higher the concentration of GP73, the better the effect of fluorescence recovery within a certain range. Therefore, by recording some known GP73 concentrations and their corresponding fluorescence recovery, the input–output relationship of the fluorescence sensing platform could be obtained. Depending on the extent of recovery of fluorescence, high sensitivity quantitative determination of GP73 is achieved.

Herein, NGQDs have good lattice fringes and good photoluminescence performance as fluorescence donors of FRET. Although the binding of GP73 aptamer to NGQDs resulted in certain lattice defects, it provided specificity for the detection of GP73. In addition, MoS2@RGO nanosheets has a strong electron transfer rate, which provides a guarantee for the quenching efficiency of fluorescence.

Furthermore, we investigated whether the NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO system was feasible to detect GP73, the fluorescence quenching and recovery experiments were investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 4B. When GP73 was not added (that is NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO system), the maximum fluorescence intensity was 635.38 (Fig. 4B, curve a). When the GP73 concentration was 500 ng/mL, the fluorescence intensity increased to 1300 (Fig. 4B, curve b) and when the concentration of GP73 was 1000 ng/mL, the fluorescence intensity increased 1930 (Fig. 4B, curve c). The phenomenon which might be explained by the specific recognition and binding of NGQDs-GP73Apt and GP73 to form the NGQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 complexes. On this condition, the interaction between NGQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2@RGO is weakened and NGQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 complexes are far away from the surface of MoS2@RGO, bringing about the recovery of fluorescence. With the increase of GP73 concentration, the degree of fluorescence recovery gradually increased. Thus, the designed fluorescence sensing platform displays positive analyte-dependent fluorescence intensity for determination target GP73.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of NGQDs-GP73Apt by MoS2@RGO nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 A shows the particle size of MoS2@RGO, NGQDs-GP73Apt, NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO and NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO-GP73 were about 396 nm, 712 nm, 255 nm and 295 nm. And the intensity distribution of the four substances increased gradually. The Zeta potential of NGQDs (Fig. 5B, curve a), NGQDs-GP73Apt (Fig. 5B, curve b), MoS2@RGO (Fig. 5B, curve c) and NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO (Fig. 5B, curve d) were −15.1 mV, −3.57 mV, −15.6 mV and −7.03 mV respectively. The Zeta potential of NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO is between NGQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2@RGO, indicating that NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO has been successfully combined.

It is well known that the concentration of fluorescent receptor (fluorescent quencher) has a great influence on the detection performance of the fluorescence sensing platform. Therefore, the concentration of the fluorescent receptor was optimized. NGQDs-GP73Apt was mixed with different concentrations of MoS2@RGO and the fluorescence emission spectra were record and showed in Fig. 5C. The corresponding fluorescence intensities were 2527, 2382, 2099, 1854, 1507, 1128, 651.8 and 621.2 when the concentration of the quencher was 0 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 200 μg/mL, 300 μg/mL, 500 μg/mL, 800 μg/mL and 1000 μg/mL, respectively. The fluorescence intensity decreased by 5.7%, 16.9%, 26.6%, 40.4%, 55.4%, 74.2% and 75.4% compared with no MoS2@RGO. There were little changes in fluorescence intensity with the concentration increased from 800 μg/mL to 1000 μg/mL, which may be because MoS2@RGO was easy to aggregate when the concentration reached a certain level, affecting its quenching effect. Therefore, the concentration of quencher selected in this experiment is 800 μg/mL.

Fig. 5D shows the time dependence of fluorescence of NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system decreased with the increase of reaction time from 0 to 20 min, which indicates that MoS2@RGO and NGQDs-GP73Apt are constantly reacting during 20 min. After 20 min of quenching, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system changed little and tended to be flat, indicating that the reaction between MoS2@RGO and NGQDs-GP73Apt is basically completed and the fluorescence quenching effect reaches the best state. Therefore, the optimum quenching time is 20 min.

For the detection process of the fluorescence sensing platform, the incubation temperature and time as well as the solution pH were optimized, respectively. All optimized values can be seen in Fig.S5. The optimum reaction conditions of NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO and GP73 were obtained: reaction temperature: 25 ℃, reaction time: 60 min, pH of PBS buffer: 7.0. These optimal values were used as the experimental conditions for further study of the fluorescence sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance and specificity of the fluorescence sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The relationship between concentration of GP73 and fluorescence recovery intensity was investigated under the most suitable experimental conditions. As can be seen from Fig. 6 A, under the condition of λex/em = 310/395 nm, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO system increased from 635 to 1341 after adding various concentrations of GP73 (from 0 ng/mL to 500 ng/mL), indicating a certain relationship between them. These may be the reason when GP73 was added to NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO system, and the intensity of fluorescence recovered due to the detachment of NGQDs-GP73Apt from MoS2@RGO nanosheets which was caused by the conformational change of the GP73Apt binding with GP73. With the increase of GP73 concentration, the relative fluorescence recovery ((F1-F0)/F0) also increased. There was a linear dependence between the relative fluorescence recovery and GP73 concentration ranging from 5.0 to 100 ng/mL (Fig. 6B). The linear expression is Y = 0.00156X + 0.00141 with the correlation coefficient of 0.99756, where ‘Y’ represents the relative fluorescence recovery (F1-F0)/F0 and ‘X’ represents the concentration of GP73, And the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 4.54 ng/mL by D = 3Sb/s (‘D’ is limit of detection, ‘3Sb’is three times the standard deviation of blank group and ‘s’ is the slope of the linear regression equation).

In addition, Table 1 shows the comparison results between the fluorescence sensing platform prepared for GP73 detection and other GP73 detection methods. Compared with ELISA, the fluorescence analysis (FRET) has lower LOD. and compared with electrochemical method, the fluorescence analysis can achieve the detection of GP73 at higher concentrations. Also, compared with fluorescence analysis using antibodies, the LOD of this fluorescence sensing platform is relatively good. And as the recognition molecule, aptamer has the advantages of lower cost and direct chemical modification, which antibodies do not have. The detection range of assays using aptamers as recognition molecules is not as good as that of antibodies, which may be related to the limited affinity of GP73 and GP73 aptamers. Therefore, the GP73 fluorescence sensing platform has the strengths of acceptably good sensitivity and selectivity, lower time and economic cost in the detection range of 5 ng/mL to100 ng/mL, providing a method and mean for the early clinical diagnosis of hepatocellular carcinoma.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and selectivity of the fluorescence sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, in order to explore the specificity of the fluorescence sensing platform for GP73, immunoglobulin G (IgG), bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA) and immunoglobulin E (IgE) were selected as interferents and compared with GP73. Fig. 6C and Fig. 6D showed the results of the specificity experiments. The fluorescence recovery intensity of GP73 to NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system is much higher than that of other different types of interferents, which reflects the specificity of this fluorescence sensing platform. The same result can be obtained by comparing the fluorescence recovery of the platform added with GP73 and mixed interferent. This is because the GP73 aptamer can accurately bind to GP73, restoring the fluorescence intensity even if other disruptors are mixed. Therefore, it can be inferred that NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO fluorescence sensor has specificity and selectivity for GP73 protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GP73 in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the feasibility of the fluorescence sensing platform based on NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system for detecting GP73 in human serum, the recovery experiments of GP73 from real human serum samples were performed. As can be seen from Table 2 . The recovery range of the fluorescence sensing platform is 97.21% − 100.83%, indicating the applicability of detecting GP73 in actual human serum samples by the platform; and the RSD range is 0.04% − 0.53%, representing that the fluorescence sensing platform has high precision. The data imply that fluorescence sensing platform based on the NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system has great application potential.

In conclusion, a fluorescence sensing platform for determination GP73 has been successfully developed based on the NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system. The fluorescent receptor MoS2@RGO nanosheets effectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-GP73Apt stemmed from FRET mechanism. After adding GP73 into NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-GP73Apt is restored as the binding affinity between GP73 and GP73Apt is stronger than the interaction between the GP73Apt and MoS2@RGO. The relative fluorescence recovery is linearly related to the concentration of GP73 in the range of 5.0 ng/mL to 100.0 ng/ mL, and the LOD is 4.54 ng/mL (S/N = 3). The GP73 fluorescence sensing platform has also been applied to the detection GP73 in human serum samples. The recovery range is 97.21% − 100.83% with RSDs of 0.04% − 0.53%. The GP73 fluorescence sensing platform based on NGQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2@RGO FRET system has the characteristics of high accuracy, wide output range, low cost and good specificity, which provides an effective and feasible fluorescence detection method for the early diagnosis of HCC and has good clinical application potential. However, due to the high sensitivity of single fluorescence detection to the environment, it is not conducive to commercialization, which will also be a problem to be solved in the future work.
The human serum samples used in this study were approved by the Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Diseases Research Ethics Committee in Guilin, China.
